skill.name: clean-code
skill.description: Applies Clean Code principles to writing, reviewing, and refactoring software for better readability, maintainability, naming, functions, comments, errors, tests, and class design.
skill.summary: Guide code review and refactoring with practical Clean Code principles.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Manual review found a Markdown-only skill with no executable scripts, runtime command calls, or network behavior. The static external command, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and network findings are false positives from inline code examples, prose, and a source attribution URL.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Inline Code Marked as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are backtick-delimited Markdown examples such as names, class examples, method names, and a small Python readability example. No instruction or file content executes these strings as Ruby, shell, or any other command language.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The affected lines are visibly Markdown prose or fenced examples, not executable code. There is no script file, command runner, or instruction to execute the examples.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Source URL Marked as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is a source attribution in the front matter. The skill does not contain fetch logic, download steps, webhooks, or instructions to send data to that URL.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 6 is metadata identifying an upstream source. A passive citation is not an operational network request or exfiltration path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern in Prose
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections occur in descriptive text and naming examples, not in cryptographic code. No MD5, SHA-1, cipher configuration, hashing function, or password storage implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss Clean Code concepts, a password-related naming example, and return codes. They do not implement or recommend a weak cryptographic primitive.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms in Design Guidance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance detections map to ordinary design guidance about names, data structures, and object access chains. No commands collect host, process, network, user, environment, or filesystem information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The terms appear inside software design examples, including Map and getter chains. There is no semantic evidence of host discovery or data collection behavior.

content.value_statement: Messy code is hard to review, extend, and test. This skill applies Clean Code principles to guide clearer names, smaller functions, better comments, safer errors, and focused classes.
actual_capabilities: Reviews names for intent, clarity, searchability, and misleading distinctions.
Applies function guidance for size, single responsibility, abstraction level, arguments, and side effects.
Evaluates comments and recommends clearer code when comments hide weak design.
Checks formatting, object boundaries, error handling, null handling, and class responsibility.
Uses a short implementation checklist for tests, function scope, arguments, and readability.
limitations: Provides guidance only; it does not run linters, tests, or static analysis tools.
Does not include language-specific style rules beyond general readability principles.
May require project context to judge tradeoffs in legacy systems or framework-heavy code.
Does not replace security review, performance profiling, or architectural design review.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests for Readability
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to identify unclear names, oversized functions, hidden side effects, and weak tests before merge.

use_cases.title: Refactor Legacy Code Safely
use_cases.description: Identify code smells and propose small readability improvements without changing expected behavior.

use_cases.title: Teach Team Code Standards
use_cases.description: Turn Clean Code principles into concrete review prompts for onboarding, pairing, and shared quality expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Readability Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small function or file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code using Clean Code principles. Focus on names, function size, comments, and obvious readability issues. Give concise suggestions.

prompt_templates.title: Refactoring Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate cleanup before changing legacy code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the Clean Code checklist to this legacy code. Identify the safest refactoring steps and explain the expected readability benefit of each step.

prompt_templates.title: Pull Request Feedback
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review for a team workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this pull request from a Clean Code perspective. Group feedback by naming, functions, error handling, tests, and class responsibility. Mark blocking issues separately.

prompt_templates.title: Design-Level Cleanup Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these related files for Clean Code design smells. Propose a phased refactoring plan that reduces coupling, improves abstraction, and preserves behavior.

output_examples.input: A function has a short unclear name, several responsibilities, and comments that restate each branch.
output: Rename the function to describe the business action it performs.
Split validation, transformation, and persistence into separate functions.
Replace redundant comments with clearer names and smaller conditional blocks.

output_examples.input: A pull request adds a large class with public fields, null returns, and no focused tests.
output: Encapsulate data behind methods that express domain intent.
Return explicit results or throw meaningful exceptions instead of returning null.
Add tests that describe the main behavior before further refactoring.

output_examples.input: A team wants a lightweight standard for everyday code review.
output_examples.output: Use a checklist covering meaningful names, small functions, limited arguments, clear errors, focused classes, and a failing test for each behavior change.

best_practices: Provide the code, its purpose, and any behavior that must not change.
Ask for prioritized feedback so the most valuable cleanup comes first.
Review suggested refactors with tests before applying them broadly.
anti_patterns: Do not request broad rewrites without explaining the behavior that must be preserved.
Do not treat Clean Code guidance as a substitute for project-specific conventions.
Do not apply naming or class changes mechanically when they reduce domain clarity.

faq.question: Does this skill modify code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides review and refactoring guidance based on Clean Code principles.

faq.question: Can it review pull requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It can structure feedback around names, functions, comments, errors, tests, and class design.

faq.question: Does it support specific programming languages?
faq.answer: It is language-agnostic. The guidance works best when you include enough code context.

faq.question: Can it find security vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on maintainability and readability, not dedicated security analysis.

faq.question: How should I use it with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: Provide a file, diff, or design concern, then ask for Clean Code feedback with priorities.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Manual review found no executable code, network behavior, or prompt injection attempts.
